card.title: How Pasteur Killed an Ancient Belief
card.teaser: For 2,000 years everyone knew maggots grew from rotten meat — until a bent glass neck proved them wrong.
card.coverImagePrompt: A 19th-century glass flask with a dramatically curved swan-neck resting on a wooden lab bench, warm candlelight, steam rising from clear broth, painterly cinematic scene

lessons.title: Life from nothing?
lessons.body: Leave meat on a shelf, and maggots appear. Leave grain in a cellar, and mice show up. For more than two millennia — from Aristotle onward — educated people believed life could simply bubble up from non-living matter when the conditions were right. They called it 'spontaneous generation,' and it seemed obvious.

visual.imagePrompt: An antique still life of rotting meat on a wooden table with flies, dark Renaissance oil painting aesthetic

lessons.title: The swan-neck idea
lessons.body: In 1859, French chemist Louis Pasteur designed an experiment so simple it sounded like a magic trick. He boiled nutrient broth in a special flask with a long, S-shaped 'swan neck' — open to the air, but curved so dust and microbes would settle in the bend instead of reaching the broth.

keyTerms.definition: The abandoned idea that living organisms can arise directly from non-living matter
A flask with a curved neck that lets air in but traps airborne particles

visual.imagePrompt: Labeled diagram of a swan-neck flask with arrows showing air entering but dust settling in the curve, textbook illustration

lessons.title: The quiet result
lessons.body: Weeks went by. The broth stayed perfectly clear — no cloudiness, no smell, no growth. Then Pasteur simply tilted one flask so the sterile broth touched the dust trapped in the curve. Within a day, it teemed with life. The conclusion was inescapable: the microbes hadn't appeared from nothing. They had ridden in on particles of dust.

visual.imagePrompt: Two swan-neck flasks side by side, one clear and pristine, one tilted and cloudy with bacterial growth, dramatic lab photography

lessons.title: A whole theory falls
lessons.body: Pasteur repeated the experiment in different places — in a Parisian cellar, on a mountainside in the Alps — and showed that air from cleaner places produced fewer contaminations. The French Academy of Sciences, which had offered a prize for settling the question, awarded it to him. Within a decade, germ theory began reshaping medicine, brewing, and food safety. Surgeons started washing their hands.
lessons.funFact: Some of Pasteur's original sealed swan-neck flasks are still on display in Paris — and the broth inside is still clear, 160 years later.

visual.imagePrompt: A bearded 19th-century scientist at a rocky Alpine outcrop, opening a flask to the thin mountain air, historic recreation

lessons.title: Why it matters
lessons.body: Pasteur didn't just debunk a bad idea — he showed how a careful experiment can overturn a belief older than Christianity. The modern idea that 'germs cause disease' rests on this shelf. Every sterile syringe and clean kitchen counter traces back to a curved glass neck.

quiz.question: What did 'spontaneous generation' claim?
options: That living things evolve over time
That life can arise from non-living matter
That diseases spread through bad air alone
quiz.explanation: The old theory held that life could bubble up from non-living material — maggots from meat, mice from grain, and so on.

quiz.question: Why was the S-shaped swan neck crucial to Pasteur's flask?
options: It trapped dust and microbes before they could reach the broth
It sealed out all air completely
It sterilized the broth with ultraviolet light
It made the broth boil at a lower temperature
quiz.explanation: The curve let air pass but forced dust and airborne microbes to settle in the bend, never touching the broth.

quiz.question: What happened when Pasteur tilted the flask so the broth touched the curve?
quiz.explanation: Once the broth met the trapped dust, microbes entered and multiplied, proving they came from the air, not from the broth.

sources.title: Mémoire sur les corpuscules organisés qui existent dans l'atmosphère
